posts.name: Ashish
posts.cooked: Taittinger Comtes de Champagne. I’ve loved all the aged ones I’ve ever had. It’s just barely within a comfortable splurge price range, so I buy a few bottles with each release.

posts.name: Nowell Karten
posts.cooked: If price isn’t an issue, then vintage Krug. To varying degrees, I’ve liked every vintage I’ve tasted (82, 85, 88, 89, 90, 95, 96, 98, 00), except the 03. Some have been spectacular.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: I am no longer sure what is actually a tete de cuvee. With the rise of grower Champagnes that rival or exceed the quality of the big houses the lines are completely blurred.

posts.name: Michael S. Monie
posts.cooked: Grower Champagnes can have tete de cuvées: e.g. Pierre Peters’ Les Chetillions.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: Understood, but there are so many different, and exclusive wines out there that I find the category is now meaningless.

posts.name: Er€z _ L£ncn€r
posts.cooked:
Michael S. Monie:

Cristal…It’s the Champagne I would love to hate, but it never disappoints. What’s yours?

You know, few weeks ago I held a mini bubbles tasting. I opened '05 Cristal, '02 Pierre Paillard, '02 Gosset celebris and '04 Bollinger Grande Année Rosé. We were five tasters and we all said (including the two that ranked the cristal at the top) that what stands for Cristal is also excellent marketing. I think two ranked the Paillard on top of the cristal, and one ranked them along side. By all means, I’d gladly drink more Cristal, but I have to say that QPR wise there are few/some additional very good options out there

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: Tasted Cristal up against Comtes de Champagne last weekend, and the CdC blew away the Cristal.

posts.name: R@y.Tupp@+sch
posts.cooked: I’m a big fan of five of the first six releases of Pol Roger SWC. For those who have included SWC in this thread, which vintages since 1990 would you put it in the top five or even ten Champagnes of the vintage?

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: 1996 for me leads the pack, 88 great but obviously a pre-1990 choice. Haven’t had enough 2008s yet.

posts.name: Fred Daniels
posts.cooked:
our house fav is Comptes, both Rose and Blanc de Blancs…if we have a windfall it’s Salon with a little age on it. But for private celebrations, the bottles that I won’t share with just anybody, it’s Grande Siecle with 10-20 years age on it. Peerless.

That said, I think we would enjoy trying just about any luxury cuvee, because it’s not as much about quality as it is exclusivity and expense. Piper Rare, Cuvee Josephine, Peters Chetillons, Crystal, they all have their interesting qualities. And I love all the different capsules!

posts.name: D@v!d P@rt@!n
posts.cooked: If I had unlimited discretionary funds I’d buy Salon and vintage Krug. Since I do not Comtes is close, sometimes equal, occasionally better. The 2006 at $100 a bottle is a legendary Champagne deal. I buy a few bottles of Cristal here and there also. Whatever the case I wouldn’t turn down any of these four.

posts.name: James Wright
posts.cooked:
one thing to keep in mind is that these têtes de cuvée are vintage wines, and thus quite dependent on the prevalent conditions of the individual year – i recall '83 Salon being rather loose and unfocussed, while the ’82 was tightly packed, generous and brilliant, and '79 like a laser beam (as was '79 Krug Clos de Mesnil).

the hot and super-ripe 1976 was rather poorly adapted to Bollinger’s RD programme, while ’75 was magnificent, for another example.

and i don’t have all that much experience with Dom P, beyond a couple memorable bottles.
perhaps Ray T. can tell us how 64 stacks up compared to 71, 73, 82…

R@y.Tupp@+sch:

I’m a big fan of five of the first six releases of Pol Roger SWC. For those who have included SWC in this thread, which vintages since 1990 would you put it in the top five or even ten Champagnes of the vintage?

I really adore the 1995 SWC

posts.name: Nowell Karten
posts.cooked:
Karl K:
Nowell Karten:

If price isn’t an issue, then vintage Krug. To varying degrees, I’ve liked every vintage I’ve tasted (82, 85, 88, 89, 90, 95, 96, 98, 00), except the 03. Some have been spectacular.

what did you not like about 2003? I have not tasted it.

I’ve only tasted in once – and maybe I caught it on a bad day or had a bad bottle – but it was awkward, disjointed, unbalanced, and altogether unpleasant, without even the pleasures of expressive, ripe fruit. I hope it gets better, as I have a bottle in storage, but I can’t say I’ve enjoyed any Champagne from 2003, including this afternoon’s taste of Dom Perignon.

posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.name: Bruce Leiser_owitz
posts.cooked:
D@v!d P@rt@!n:

If I had unlimited discretionary funds I’d buy Salon and vintage Krug. Since I do not Comtes is close, sometimes equal, occasionally better. The 2006 at $100 a bottle is a legendary Champagne deal. I buy a few bottles of Cristal here and there also. Whatever the case I wouldn’t turn down any of these four.

As I posted the other week, a local grocery store chain was having its semi-annual clear the shelves sale. While many stores carried the same inventories, some individual stores had their own selection. One of my local stores had the 2006 Taittinger Comtes for only $90/bottle including tax. Best deal I’ve seen so far on that one.

I don’t have a single favorite Champagne, as it seems to be a function of the mood I’m in. Sometimes I drink Champagne and think it’s amazing nectar of the gods and wonder why I don’t drink more of it. Other times, it just seems like white wine with bubbles in it. So, for example, I can have some Krug on one night and be blown away; on another night it really doesn’t float my boat that much. Since I don’t think there’s that much bottle variation, I think it’s more of an issue of my personal mood.

Bruce

Many thanks to Jay Hack who opened one at one of our blind dinners which convinced me to buy a few.

posts.name: stewart lancaster
posts.cooked: I am somewhat a novice when it comes to this area of champagne. Would like to try several over the holidays. Can you recommend several under $50?-or is that price too low?

posts.name: Michael S. Monie
posts.cooked:
stewart lancaster:

I am somewhat a novice when it comes to this area of champagne. Would like to try several over the holidays. Can you recommend several under $50?

While I’m sure some here will disagree, Champagne under $50 is getting difficult to obtain. From a big house I’d look for Laurent-Perrier Brut. And for a grower I’d recommend Aubry Brut.

stewart lancaster:

I am somewhat a novice when it comes to this area of champagne. Would like to try several over the holidays. Can you recommend several under $50?-or is that price too low?

Stewart, the thread is about the tete cuvees, which are almost without exception more than $50, and in almost every case 2 or 3 times that much. The closest to $50 I imagine you will get to a great tete cuvee experience is the 1999 Billecart-Salmon Nicolas Francois. It is a fantastic champagne, which for reasons I can’t really explain has periodically been available over the last couple of years for $69. The cheapest source I can recommend at present is $85 (see link below). Note that the more recent vintages have been priced in the $145/150 range on release; I don’t think you will ever see this wine in the sub-$100 range again, from any vintage.

https://www.benchmarkwine.com/wines/31707-billecart-salmon-nicolas-francois-1999

None of this is to say that there aren’t fantastic champagnes available in the $50 and under price point. You just aren’t going to find a representative “house’s best” in that range

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked: the original question is, of course, inseperable from cost issues so I’ll also say Comte which is my single largest holding of Champagne (not necessarily the Champagne I buy the most but certainly the one I hold onto the longest). I’ve often preferred it to more expensive cuvees when tasted side by side.

posts.name: R@y.Tupp@+sch
posts.cooked:
James Wright:

and i don’t have all that much experience with Dom P, beyond a couple memorable bottles.
perhaps Ray T. can tell us how 64 stacks up compared to 71, 73, 82…

'64 surely is as good as those vintages, although most original releases are on their downward slope. '47, '49, '52, '55, '59, '61, '62, '64, '66, '69, '71, '73, and '76 DP is why I put Dom Perignon as my favorite Champagne. My experience with prior vintages is limited and most that I’ve had were well past their prime.

posts.name: Rob Sutherland
posts.cooked:
D@vid Bu3ker:

Understood, but there are so many different, and exclusive wines out there that I find the category is now meaningless.

Agree. Also with the advent of so many mono-site wines which, I think by definition can’t be a Tete de Cuvée, you are not capturing all of the ‘best’ wines from a producer anymore.

That said, Heidsieck BdM is probably my favorite of the true Tete de Cuvée on a QPR basis through Taittinger Comtes and Philipponat CdG are always fantastic as well.

posts.name: scott c
posts.cooked: Clos des Goisses. I have questions for those who have mentioned Salon and Krug. The first is almost philosophical: if Salon only makes one wine, is it really a tete de cuvee? As for Krug, what wine are you saying is the tete de cuvee? If you ask Krug, they will tell you that they take as much, if not more, pride in the MV than the vintage. By price, both pale in comparison to the single vineyards.

posts.name: Will P@yne
posts.cooked:
Nowell Karten:
Karl K:
Nowell Karten:

If price isn’t an issue, then vintage Krug. To varying degrees, I’ve liked every vintage I’ve tasted (82, 85, 88, 89, 90, 95, 96, 98, 00), except the 03. Some have been spectacular.

what did you not like about 2003? I have not tasted it.

I’ve only tasted in once – and maybe I caught it on a bad day or had a bad bottle – but it was awkward, disjointed, unbalanced, and altogether unpleasant, without even the pleasures of expressive, ripe fruit. I hope it gets better, as I have a bottle in storage, but I can’t say I’ve enjoyed any Champagne from 2003, including this afternoon’s taste of Dom Perignon.

That’s truly disappointing. Krug is probably my favorite Champagne producer and I have an 03 stashed away for my upcoming birthday.

Here’s to hoping it’s bottle variation…

posts.name: Rob Sutherland
posts.cooked:
scott c:

Clos des Goisses. I have questions for those who have mentioned Salon and Krug. The first is almost philosophical: if Salon only makes one wine, is it really a tete de cuvee? As for Krug, what wine are you saying is the tete de cuvee? If you ask Krug, they will tell you that they take as much, if not more, pride in the MV than the vintage. By price, both pale in comparison to the single vineyards.

I would call Salon the tete de cuvee and Delamotte the non.
If you were going to be a stickler I think single vineyard champagnes by definition can’t be a tete de cuvee so for Krug you have the Vintage as the tete de cuvee, the MV as the regular bottling and the two others as outliers. Similar to Bollinger with Grande Annee being the Tete de cuvee and not the VVF.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Though it’s probably my favorite wine in Champagne these days, I would query classifying Clos des Goisses as a tête de cuvée on the grounds that it’s a single-vineyard wine.

Post-1990 I’d have give serious consideration to Taittinger Comtes, Charles Heidseick BdM and Pierre Péters Les Chetillons, with Krug vintage probably winning the top spot. The '02 and '04 Salon are not making the same music as the 1996 so I don’t think they could get my vote.

posts.name: Warren Taranow
posts.cooked:
stewart lancaster:

I am somewhat a novice when it comes to this area of champagne. Would like to try several over the holidays. Can you recommend several under $50?-or is that price too low?

NV Coutier Blanc de Blanc. Not a tete de cuvée, but Grand Cru and fantastic at about $40 per bottle. It’s what I open when I want a great BdB but don’t want to open one of my Comte. One third the price, but way better than 1/3 the quality.

Warren Taranow:
stewart lancaster:

I am somewhat a novice when it comes to this area of champagne. Would like to try several over the holidays. Can you recommend several under $50?-or is that price too low?

NV Coutier Blanc de Blanc. Not a tete de cuvée, but Grand Cru and fantastic at about $40 per bottle. It’s what I open when I want a great BdB but don’t want to open one of my Comte. One third the price, but way better than 1/3 the quality.

the rose is no slouch either

Hard to argue against Dom from the 60s, 70s and 80s, at least the ones I’ve tasted, which were admittedly from the best years from those decades.

One random discovery tasted side-by-side with the same vintage of Ruinart BdB (long a personal favorite) - the Pierre Moncuit 2004 ‘Cuvee Nicole Moncuit’. Comes from a total of 1.5 acres of 90-year-old vineyards. Killer stuff, beat the Ruinart at the table blind and costs a lot less. Lower dosage than Ruinart, but more complex.

Comtes for me too - I’m surprised how strong the support is even though I agree.

No surprises in the others - Krug, Dom, Ruinart BdB. I have a bit of Salon but I haven’t dug in. Old LGD can be surprisingly good. I’m optimistic on Peters Chetillons but don’t think I’ve had one older than 05 (and I know they don’t go a lot further back).

posts.name: scott c
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

Though it’s probably my favorite wine in Champagne these days, I would query classifying Clos des Goisses as a tête de cuvée on the grounds that it’s a single-vineyard wine.

Post-1990 I’d have give serious consideration to Taittinger Comtes, Charles Heidseick BdM and Pierre Péters Les Chetillons, with Krug vintage probably winning the top spot. The '02 and '04 Salon are not making the same music as the 1996 so I don’t think they could get my vote.

If you’re disqualifying Goisses, you have to disqualify Chetillons, no?

posts.name: scott c
posts.cooked:
Rob_S:
scott c:

Clos des Goisses. I have questions for those who have mentioned Salon and Krug. The first is almost philosophical: if Salon only makes one wine, is it really a tete de cuvee? As for Krug, what wine are you saying is the tete de cuvee? If you ask Krug, they will tell you that they take as much, if not more, pride in the MV than the vintage. By price, both pale in comparison to the single vineyards.

I would call Salon the tete de cuvee and Delamotte the non.

That wouldn’t be entirely accurate. They are sister companies under the same corporate umbrella. If you want to get particular, I’ll hear arguments that Salon is the tete de cuvee of Laurent-Perrier, but I think that would be equally silly.

Similar to Bollinger with Grande Annee being the Tete de cuvee and not the VVF.

Except the VVF is not a single vineyard.
